speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Eli Benyacar-- this is tape six. You received these false papers, which you bought.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came to Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your new identity? Who did you become?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became Efraim Mucher. And I-- and then I was successful, and I got my visa. And the Jewish Congress brought me over. I came with about 70 young kids, all same age. We were all over 18, but we'd all made papers under 18. Some people changed their name. Some people changed the ages. Some people changed their countries, to just to be able to come to Canada, or through the Jewish Congress. So we were about 70 kids. We had boys and girls, and we were very-- we were very lucky. We got into an American boat. The name of the boat as We were about 9 or 10 days, on the ocean. We arrived in Halifax. There, we were-- they received us with open arms, the Jewish people in Halifax. They really were happy to see us. And it was June 23. And then they-- and in the evening, after we arrived, they give us the stamp that we are embarking-- embarkation stamp. They put us into trains. As a matter of fact, they looked us very good. They put us into trains with sleepers on them, to sleep on the train from Halifax to Montreal. In Montreal, another group of Jewish people came in. They look after us there for a while. And the next evening, again, they put us into train from Montreal to Toronto. In those days, it used to take about 10 or 12 hours. We also had sleepers. And the first time I saw a colored person was the porter-- a porter on the train, a big black guy who woke us up in the morning. We got into the Toronto Union Station. And sure enough, a bunch of Jewish kids, Canadian Jewish kids, came in with their parents, with their cars. And they took us, and they brought us into what you call on Markham Street. There used to be the Jewish center on Markham Street.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- the-- there was a lot of rooms down there which, today, is a library. And-- but there was no room for me at the-- what do you call? At the center there. They put me in the Beverley Hotel on Spadina for a few nights, till a few other kids moved out of the center. Then I moved-- I moved back into the-- into the center, from the hotel.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the mean time, I stayed about four or five nights in the Beverley Hotel on Spadina and College. From there, they were trying to find homes for us to live on, because the center-- more kids were coming in, bi-weekly or bi-monthly. You know, they were coming, a lot of kids from all over, all over the world, actually-- from England, from many places. And they found me-- I found a house by the name-- [INAUDIBLE] used to be-- on College Street, they used to have-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what do you call-- making suits. Anyway, a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tailors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tailors-- [INAUDIBLE] tailor on College Street. And it was a very nice family, and I-- and the Congress was giving me $18 a week to live on, in those days. And I used to pay $14 for room and board for the lady. And $4, I used to have for spare money.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the meantime, I had quite a few dollars when I came over. I was the richest kid of all the 70, you know? I had a lot of what I sold in stores in Germany, I made into dollars. I brought in 50 watches, an accordion, a typewriter. You name it, I brought it-- a Leica camera. I brought everything with me, so I wasn't, you know, suffering.

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, but after two months of being around, you know, everybody-- all my friends went to work, except me. I found a nice lady here, a friend, a cousin of a friend of mine, and she-- Lucy Goldstein, nice lady. She died. And she was from Greece, but born in the United States. But her parents were from Greece.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, well, she got acquainted. She got to-- you know, got to like me. She says, OK, [INAUDIBLE]. Says, what do you want to do now? I says, I want to make money. My friends were working for $18 a week, $18, $19, $0.45 an hour. And I was getting $18 from the Congress [INAUDIBLE] working, you know? So finally, I says, I want to make more money. So finally got me a job, six days a week, at Canada Bread, making doughnuts, and $35 an hour-- $35 a week. It was almost double what my friends were making. But it was double the hard work that I was working there, making doughnuts by hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And over there, I-- I learned the English language. I was the only Jew ever worked in this particular factory, and nobody knew I was Jewish. And I had all Irish people working there, bakers-- old people, 60, 65. And they were all, you know, good people, all old people. I was the youngest kids there, working in a bakery. What I was doing, I don't know. In the meantime, they were teaching me English. Lunchtime, we used to go for lunch all together. And we used to say, this is a fork. This is a spoon. You know, they were teaching me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I got to like it there, you know? I learned quite a bit. While my friends, they're still learning the ABC, I really was making full statements in English. You know, like I could get picked up. And they got to-- get to like me very nicely. Everybody liked me.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the foreman, like Sundays-- we used to work Sundays. I-- I typed a letter, a song, and I made copies of it. And I put it in every part of the factory. Cruising down the river on Sunday afternoon, because we used to work on a Sunday. So I used to have all the guys singing and [INAUDIBLE]. You know, it was sort of a levity, a happy occasion. Then when I brought my brother over in 1949, I took a month off. When I went back, they want to put me to night shift. Oh, night shift, I ain't working. So I quit.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got my brother a job. He was a typewriter mechanic, so he got a job nicely, about $25 a week. But in the meantime, they got to like him. He became very-- he worked there for 30 years, till he retired there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in the meantime, me, I didn't know what to do with myself. So finally, I want make money. So I went to work as a presser, you know, in a big garment-- by the name, a guy, Mr. Schwartz. And he was a tough son of a gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was making good money and-- but a lot of guys were making $30, $40 a week. I was making $100. But I was working hard, from 7:00 in the morning to 7:00 in the evening. But I didn't care. I was young, you know? As long as I can see a result. But that wasn't a job for me. Finally-- because I already start pick up the English language. Then I had a friend of mine who got into Halifax. What do you call? Ziegler, Roman Ziegler-- he's a Polish Jew. And he came from Halifax. He went to Niagara Falls, and he was living there. And he came to Toronto. He says, oh, you know, some of these opportunities in [INAUDIBLE] and Niagara Falls. You speak Italian, you know? We can do some business. I says, OK. I went over there for a couple of days with him. Were some stuff-- some jobs here. And we went a couple of suitcases, so house to house.

speaker: interviewee
text: We started selling quite a bit of stuff, making a few bucks. But I said, that's not a job for me, as going with suitcases. So I had saved money. I bought myself a truck, a panel truck, for $1,510-- brand-new truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I drove to Niagara Falls, and we-- I was coming Sundays, do some buying. And Monday and Tuesday, I used to started going to houses. And I used to wait for the [INAUDIBLE], just to come into their houses, to collect the [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] $2 down, $2 a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that didn't appeal to me. I stayed there for about nine months-- eight, nine months. And I says to Roman, I says, Roman, one person can make a good living here. But for two of us, we don't need two guys to sell the same stuff. You know, [INAUDIBLE] the same. You want it, you can have it. If not, I'll take it. [INAUDIBLE] you, you are in Toronto. I came from Halifax. I don't know anybody in Toronto. Says, OK, Roman. You own it. I says, when you have the money, you pay me. So the finale was he took it over, and then he paid me for the truck. But he didn't last very long.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he give it up. So he went to work for Tip Top Tailors as a salesman. So in the meantime, I came back to Toronto. My boss found out that I'm on the loose again-- the guy, the presser. And he says, please come and help me. I need you right now. I said, but I don't want to be a presser. [INAUDIBLE]. He says, you can quit anytime. No-- don't give me any notices. But I do need you right now, you know? I says, OK, Mr. Schwartz. I'll come to work. So I worked there for a couple of weeks, or three weeks [INAUDIBLE] the season. And all of a sudden, I see an ad in the paper-- salesman wanted, at an army-navy store, 150 King Street East. So I go over there on a Wednesday. I was-- with a nice gaberdine coat on me. And I says, I'm looking at the ad. I says-- my English was so-- you know, it's only two years here, 1950, '51. How much can you learn in such a short time, English? So the guy was the son there. The father wasn't-- the owner wasn't there. The son was there. So he takes a looks at me and says, OK. Give me your phone number. I'll call you back. He was younger than me. I says to him, I says, are you looking for phone numbers, or are you looking for somebody to work? The guy didn't give an answer. I says, where's your broom? He showed me the broom. So I took the broom. I start sweeping the floors, you know? I says, no, I don't want no pay. I says, I'll work for nothing. Just want to-- so I hired myself. I worked there Wednesday afternoon, Thursday, Friday, and Saturday.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden on Saturday a big slob comes in. A big, fat guy comes in, takes a look at me. But I didn't know the names. Like sleeping bags-- I didn't know what a sleeping bag is, or a camp cot. They were selling camping stuff. But pants, shirts, boots, shoes, socks, gloves, I knew. But this stuff, I didn't even know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they had another guy, a salesman over there. If a guy would ask me for something I don't know, I used to transfer it to him. And then I was-- because I was quick. So the old man comes in, says, who are you? I says, my name-- my name Al. I work here. Oh, I'm the boss. How do you do, sir? You know? [INAUDIBLE]. So the guy [INAUDIBLE]. He says, go to my car. I got some boxes. Bring it in. So anyways, Saturday afternoon comes up, in the evening, 6:00. He says, how much you want to get paid? Well, I says, I don't want to get paid much. I says, just to eat. He says, how much you want? I says, $50 a week. Those days, were working for $35. A big salesman was making $40. I asked for $50. So the guy says to me, what kind of food you eat? [LAUGHS] You know? He was sharp. He was a New Yorker, a real character. But he thought. He says, OK, how much I owe you? I says, $32. So he gives me $35. That was it. I work for him. I made him a lot of money. I worked for him for 10 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made him a lot of money. I bought him a Jaguar, and I sent him to Europe, because I really went into the business. And I was the business already. But I was getting paid very well. He paid me-- I, when I quit this job in 1961, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was making over $20,000 clear, tax paid, plus all the benefits for the house. You know, when I got married, I was working there. He brought me a dining room set. He-- you know, he was very good to me. And I made a lot of money there, and I saved it. You know, and then I bought a little house on Marquette Street. And it was very nice. I worked there for 10 years. I can see I'm getting big pay, and I'm getting everything. But it's no future for me here. I'm still going to be a working guy. And I asked my boss, because I want to open more stores, and I want to go into the wholesale business.

speaker: interviewee
text: I asked Lou, I says, listen. Let me buy a half, or let me buy one third-- one third to your son, one third to you, and one third to me. But I want to have something on paper. I'm traveling to New York, to Chicago, different places. Sometimes, you know, your airplane can fall-- fall. And I'm die, what is my kids going to have? What my wife's going to have?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] he's sort of mellow. And then his wife got mixed in. I says, well, this is a family [INAUDIBLE]. I says, OK, that's the case. I says, Lou, I'm quitting. Here's the keys. I give you three weeks' notices.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, OK. [INAUDIBLE] keep your keys. When three weeks come by, then I quit. And I didn't prepare myself at all. I was completely out of-- I was lost. Here I was, a busy man. I was working seven days a week, 70, 80 hours. But I was getting paid, you know? I was making good money for me, and for him too. And all of a sudden, I'm out of a job. And I am miserable, real miserable. So I couldn't sleep. I went to bed, couldn't sleep. Then I went to New York, to the [INAUDIBLE]. I went to eat at a butcher shop. And went over there. I told him. He said, what are you worried about? He said, come over here. So I couldn't go to the States anyway. So I got a couple of drinks, and I went back to Toronto. I came back to Toronto. And there was a store across the street. Then I was sore that they rejected me. You know, they reject the offer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a store across the street, also an army. It was for sale. So I went. I was going to buy it and be in competition with them. And I went to the bank. And I-- Sunday night, I took stock of this [INAUDIBLE] market, [INAUDIBLE] the store.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took stock, and there was $40,000 worth of inventory. And the bank-- I went to the bank next day, which was across the street. [INAUDIBLE] Bank of Commerce made a certified check to go get the store. Would you believe it? I didn't have the guts, the strength, to walk from this sidewalk to this sidewalk, which was the store. I said to myself, I'm doing something wrong. I'm doing something wrong, because I'll be competition with my old boss. He was so good to me. He was so good to my kids. And I'm going to go, and I'm going to kill him, you know? Go across the street-- I'm doing something wrong. So I-- I didn't go across. I went into the car. I went, on Young Street, to a tavern. And I had two doubles of Crown Royal on the rocks. And I went in my car. I fall asleep. And around 6:00, I woke up, and I come home. My wife says, what happened to you, dear? The guy's looking for you. He's phoning the whole day long. I says, honey-- I says, you know something? I'm doing something wrong. There's no way that I should buy this business. Well, OK-- says, you don't want it? Go and tell the guy. So the next day I went. I took three $100 bills. I give him $300. I says, here's for the trouble I give you, for Sunday that we took your stock. I says, I'm doing the wrong thing by buying this store. I don't want to go competition with my ex-boss, which he was good to me. He realized that. He took the $300, and I was happy. And I started doing business from my basement of the house, you know? I started selling typewriters to lawyers and doctors. I had a friend in Montreal. He says, hell, I'll send you some typewriters, used electric typewriters.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll give you, $50 apiece. You sell them for $99. Put a little ad in the paper, classified ads. You'll see. And I used to get teachers [INAUDIBLE] my house on Marquette Street. But I was doing very well. Next, I started going into New York, buying surplus, back. And I went into the surplus business. And I had a lot of connections with the American boys. When I used to work for the store, everybody thought that I owned the store. And I was one of the most well-liked buyer in the United States, in the surplus field.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then when I went into business for myself, I-- somebody gave me a cold storage room free of rent, and a little desk at an office down there. So-- and I went to New York, to Chicago, which was a very close friend of mine, that I used to buy stuff from him when I used to work for the store. And he saw me disappointed. Says, oh, I want to quit my job. He says, what are you worried about? Says, come work for me. How much were you making there? Here's an office, because he knew how good I was. I says, listen. How can I come? I have the kids, a family in Toronto, and all that. So anyway, said, what do you want to do? I says, I want to be an importer. He was an importer at the time. Says, you got money? Says, yeah. How much you got? Says, I got $50,000. In those days, it's a lot of money. OK, let's go for dinner. We went out for dinner, and he was buying stuff from Japan in those days.

speaker: interviewee
text: At those times, there was no [INAUDIBLE]. This was in 1960, was everything phone. So well, let's wait till 10:00 at night. We call Japan, and we give them the order. You know, I knew what [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So we called the agent in Japan. He says, OK. This and this, you ship it to Canada. But he was paying a 1 and 1/2% commission to the agent in Japan. So I says, you're going to be given 3% commission, which was very good-- buying out his buying power, you know? It's getting same price as his.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when I started my importing business, but I still had the surplus. Surplus was in my blood. And I worked with him, for this, since 1961. And I started importing goods, and I move into a 3,000 square feet of space, doing very well. And then I became quite active. I used to go to Japan and Taiwan three times a year, you know, traveling, buying goods. And I got involved with Canadian Tire, the buyer. You know, I got to know the buyer very well. And actually, Canadian Tire made me what I am today, to start out with. And I rented a place, 3,000 square feet, on Davis. And then I took the whole building on Davis. Finally, the business was going good, very successful. And then I went to Israel in 1961. In 1960, before I quit, I went to Israel. And I had-- my boss paid the fare and everything else. Well, when I came back in June, July, I started my own business. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you got married. When did you get married?

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Esther, Esther [NON-ENGLISH], which is we've been married for 43 years, so very successful-- very good woman, lovely woman. She stood by me all the time, no arguments. And we-- then my son started getting a little older, my big boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have three children, two boys and a girl. The oldest is the girl, Diane, which they all went through the associate school. They're all-- my daughter is a Hebrew teacher. My two boys speak, fluently, Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are the names of your sons?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son is Michael and Ian, and Diane is my girl, which-- the grandchild you just saw here, this is my daughter's oldest. We had her bat mitzvah a year ago. And from there on, we built the first building on Gordon Baker Road. It was a nice-size building, around 65,000 square feet-- business improving all the time. And then my son got a little older. I sent him to Japan to do some buying when he was 18 years old, by himself, which his mother was going to kill me. But I says, the only way he'll learn, if he goes on his own. With me, it won't be any good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he became-- my son, today, goes to the Orient for the last few years. As a matter of fact, he just came last night, from the Orient. And we outgrow the place. We built the place in Gordon Baker Road in 1974. And by 1990, we have no more room.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had two storage places, many places, business grow up, you know-- doing very, very well, till I build this new building on-- in Concord, which is 145,000 square feet. And we're doing very well. I employ about 40 people, and I still work every day. And I'm healthy, and I-- I don't know if I-- if I missed anything. But Canada has been very good to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you first came over to Canada, did you talk about your experiences to anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, because we were all-- when we came here, we were all the same, when I came with my friends. My friends were all survivors. I have nothing to talk about. And if you talk to-- if you were to talk to any Canadian, they won't believe it anyway. What they know?

speaker: interviewer
text: When we came over here, they thought we are greenhorns. But in the meantime, the Canadians that were here, they were the greenhorns, because we-- they took us to a house. Says, oh, you know, this is a radio. You see? You know, you-- comes the voice, comes from a radio-- like we didn't know. We knew what a radio was. One of my friends says, is there somebody underneath it that talking to-- you know? The people that were here-- the people that were here, they came in the 18th-- in 1918. They didn't have anything, you know? And whatever they had over here, they thought that still, in Europe, it's still the same. But Europe wasn't the same. We had the same thing in Europe that the people in Toronto had, you know? But they-- and those were non-educated people. They all left because they didn't want to go to the army, or they have pogroms.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had pogroms in Poland, in Ukrainian, and all that. They'd all run away. They came over here. They were all-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] working here for $4 a week, working tailors, and so on. But some of the people were nice. And some of the people, they thought that we-- we are coming in from where they came. But we-- we already saw so much during the war that our eyes-- we became experienced in almost-- in anything. You take it in the countries around-- surround us, Europe surround us, the food, the electricity, the radios, the TVs. No, we have no TVs. I mean the telephones, et cetera, et cetera. When I came to this country here, they had iceboxes. You know, they didn't have fridges yet. It was-- the guy used to sell a cube of ice box to put in the-- to put in the iceboxes, in 1948. And they were still selling bread with a horse and wagon. It used to come in the houses, and same way with the milk. But we were-- we got adapted. I got adapted very well, into Canadian life. And so did all my friends. All my friends did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your experiences of the war affect the way you live your life today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm happy every day I get up in the morning. I'm very happy to get up in the morning. To me, it's an extra life-- that I got an extra life ahead of me, because as far as I'm concerned, I could have been in the gas chamber in 1943, on April 5. Secondly if I wouldn't be lucky in concentration camp, I would have been dead, because I could not take the labor or the cold weather that we had, and the miserable things that we went through at the lager. You know, if I wouldn't be lucky there, then I wouldn't be alive today. And as far as I'm concerned, I have a second life. And every day that I am here-- we call it, in Polish, [POLISH]. A [POLISH] means an extra portion of food. When we used to be in concentration camp, on a Wednesday they used to give us, let's say-- or a Thursday-- an extra piece of bread. And in Auschwitz-- in Birkenau, was strictly Polish. And [INAUDIBLE] he says, tonight we have a [POLISH], which is an extra piece of bread they were giving us to eat. But to me, [POLISH] means an extra life. So that's-- that's as far as I'm-- as far as Canada is concerned, I am very happy to be alive. And I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: You lost your parents, and your sisters, and one brother to the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I survive? Luck, age-- age, luck, and a little bit of [NON-ENGLISH], a little bit of brains, that I-- I didn't give up. I didn't give up. I don't say-- because a lot of people give up. They went to the electric wire, touched it, and they got burned themselves. But I said, tomorrow's another day. I didn't give up. I always hold myself to it. And that's why I survived. And on top of that, as I told you before, I did not work. The-- I only work very short times, only in transits when we were moving from one camp to the other. I had a hard times.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as soon as I got established in a camp, I was-- I always manage to have a good job, you know? And the-- the job and the food meant survival, because I never got sick. And there was one day in the-- the only thing I used to having in the [INAUDIBLE], in camp, was some boils used to come in, or [NON-ENGLISH], which is this-- you get from the lice, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like you scratch yourself. But you put something on it, it goes away. That's the only thing I had in camp. But a lot of people had many, many sickness, they developed. And I didn't, which I was very lucky. Again, it's luck. Luck, it meant a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to go to break now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
